HANDBALL AND ITS DEVELOPMENT

SOME SUGGESTIONS.

To the Editor "Gaelic Athlete."

COLLEGIANS IN COUNCIL

The general annual meeting of the College of Hurling Club was held at 96 St. Stephen's Green on Tuesday night inst., Rev. T. Corcoran, S.J., D. Litt., presiding. After a review of last season's working, the following officers were elected:

President, Rev. T. Corcoran; Captain of Senior Team, John Ryan (University College); Vice-captain of Senior Team, Con Burke (College of Science); Hon. Sec. and Treas., Con O'Donovan (College of Science).

In reply to a vote of thanks, the Rev. Chairman exhorted all to "do their bit" for the grand old national pastime and, profiting by the experience gained during the last two years, to leave nothing undone this year that would help to bring the club even more to the forefront in Dublin hurling circles.

We trust that the exhortation of the Rev. Chairman will have the desired effect on the "Collegians." There is no reason whatever to debar the students of the College of Science from becoming an effective force among the foremost elements in Dublin Gaolom.

MINORS IN MOTION.

The meeting of Lamb Dhearg and Fontenoyes for the final of the Minor Championship produced a highly interesting game. "Fontes" won the toss, and, starting with the wind, scored a point after three minutes' play, almost immediately adding a goal to their credit. However, "Lamb's" made headway, and registered a goal before the interval. Assisted by the breeze in the second period, Lamb Dhearg gave a much better exhibition than in the opening match, J. Murphy equalising from ten yards puck. Soon after "Lamb's" took over the run of the game, adding four goals to their credit, thereby leaving themselves the better team. Before the final whistle, "Fontes" scored a goal and "Lamb's" a point, the latter retiring easy winners by 5-2 to 2-1.

NEW YORK

It is surprising that nobody don't come along and suggest a date or meeting place for the governing body and Big Four teams to meet and thresh out the grievances or grievances which hinders them from coming together.
